14: ti ophelos adelphoi mou ean pistin lege tis echein erga de me eche (ti ophelos, adelphoi mou, ean pistin legē tis echein, erga de mē echē): 'What good is it, my brothers, if someone says he has faith but does not have works?' The famous James-Paul tension: Luther notoriously called James 'an epistle of straw' for seeming to contradict Paul's faith-righteousness. The resolution lies in different uses of pistis: Paul's 'faith' is the whole person's trust in and surrender to God; James's target is a merely verbal profession (legē tis echein, 'says he has faith') without ethical corollary. James and Paul agree that genuine faith produces works (cf. Gal 5:6: 'faith working through love'); their different opponents require different emphases.
1: prosōpolēmpsiais — favoritism (prosōpolēmpsiais, plural of prosōpolēmpsia) is a compound: prosōpon (face) + lambanō (to receive/take). The LXX translates the Hebrew nasa panim ('to lift the face') with this compound — to accept/respect someone based on their outward appearance or social position. The OT prohibits this in legal and social contexts (Deut 1:17; 10:17; Lev 19:15). James applies the prohibition to the assembly context. The plural (prosōpolēmpsiais) is unusual — perhaps emphasizing the many specific instances that constitute the attitude.
2: synagōgē — James uses synagōgē (synagogue/assembly) for the Christian meeting, not ekklēsia (the more common NT word for church). This is one of the only NT uses of synagōgē for a Christian gathering (cf. also Heb 10:25's episynagōgē). It may indicate the Jewish-Christian character of James's audience — they still used the Jewish vocabulary for their assembly. chrysodaktylios (gold-ringed) is a compound hapax: wearing a gold ring on the finger was a sign of equestrian rank in Rome; it signals wealth at a glance.
3: epiblepō — you look with special regard (epiblepsēte, aorist subjunctive of epiblepō) on the man in fine clothes. The verb means 'to look upon with special attention' — it can be positive (God looking favorably on the humble, Luke 1:48) or here negative (looking with preference on the wealthy). The spatial metaphor continues: 'here is a good seat for you' vs 'stand there' or 'sit at my feet' (hypokato tou hypopodio mou, under my footstool) — the foot-position is an extreme social degradation, normally used for slaves or conquered enemies.
4: diekrithēte — you have discriminated/made distinctions (diekrithēte, passive of diakrinō). The same root as diakrisis (discernment); here it is making distinctions that should not be made — social sorting within the covenant community. 'Judges with evil thoughts' (kritai dialogismōn ponērōn) — dialogismos usually means reasoning/thought but here has the sense of judicial reasoning that is ponērōn (evil/corrupt). They have set themselves up as judges and their judging principle is corrupt because it mirrors the world's social hierarchy.
5: tous ptōchous tō kosmō, plousious en pistei — the Greek antithesis is sharp: poor in the world (ptōchous tō kosmō, dative of sphere — poor with respect to the world's measure), rich in faith (plousious en pistei). The poor whom the world despises are the rich ones in God's economy, heirs of the kingdom (klēronomous tēs basileias). Klēronomos (heir) is a legal term for one who inherits by right; James's claim is that the poor who love God have a legal claim on the kingdom.
6: ētimāsate — you have dishonored (ētimāsate, aorist of atimazō). The root is timē (honor); atimazō is to strip of honor, to treat as without worth. The indictment is that the Jamesian community has done exactly what the world does: respected wealth and stripped honor from poverty, precisely reversing the divine evaluation of v5. The rhetorical questions that follow (v6b, v7) build the irony: the wealthy whom you honor are the very ones dragging you into courts and blaspheming the name.
7: to kalon onoma to epiklēthen eph' hymas — 'the noble name called over you' (to kalon onoma to epiklēthen eph' hymas). The phrase 'the name called over' comes from the LXX formula epikaleō to onoma kyriou — the name of YHWH invoked over Israel as his possession (Deut 28:10; Amos 9:12; Acts 15:17). To have the name called over you is to be claimed as belonging to the one whose name it is. Blaspheming this name is not merely insulting Christians but attacking the Lord to whom they belong. Kalon (noble/beautiful/good) emphasizes the worth of the name being defamed.
8: nomos basilikos — the royal law (nomos basilikos, literally 'kingly law'). The title is unique in the NT. It likely means the supreme law that governs the whole of covenant life — the royal commandment that functions as the organizing principle of all other law. James identifies it as the Lev 19:18 command ('Love your neighbor as yourself'), the same text Jesus identified as the 'great commandment' (Matt 22:39; Mark 12:31). The kata tēn graphēn (according to the Scripture) grounds the command in its OT locus rather than in Jesus's citation alone.
9: prosōpolēmpteite... elenchomenoi hypo tou nomou hōs parabatai — if you show partiality, you commit sin (hamartian ergazesthe, you work/produce sin — the same verb as 1:20 'produce righteousness') and are convicted (elenchomenoi — to be shown up, exposed, convicted by evidence) by the law as transgressors (parabatai, those who step over the boundary/violate). The legal register is prominent: the law functions as prosecutor that demonstrates the violation. The person who shows favoritism has violated the royal law of v8, making them a lawbreaker rather than a keeper.
10: holōn nomon tērein... heni de ptaisē — whoever keeps the whole law but stumbles (ptaisē, from ptaiō — to trip/stumble, used for a misstep rather than a fall; the same word in v2 and 3:2) at one point has become liable (gegonan enachos) for all. The legal logic is that Torah is a unified system, not a collection of independent rules — its integrity depends on the unity of the lawgiver (v11: the one who said both prohibitions is the same God). Violating any commandment is a relational offense against the unified will of the one God, not merely a statistical infraction.
11: ho eipon... eipen — the same one who said ('you shall not commit adultery') also said ('you shall not murder'). The argument grounds the unity of the law in the unity of the lawgiver: God is the single will behind both commandments, so violating one is an offense against the whole person of God, not merely that single rule. The order — adultery mentioned before murder — is unusual (normally murder first, cf. Exod 20:13-14); it may be the order of Deut 5:17-18 or simply James's order, possibly citing the commandment seen as more 'minor' (adultery) before the obviously serious one (murder) to underscore that both carry equal weight.
12: dia nomou eleutherias krinesthai — to be judged through the law of freedom (dia nomou eleutherias). The same phrase as 1:25 (nomos teleios tēs eleutherias). James frames judgment in terms of the 'perfect law of liberty' — not the law as enslaving code but the word of truth (1:18, 21) that liberates from disordered desire. Speaking and acting should be done in the awareness that the criterion of final judgment is this freedom-producing law: have you lived and spoken in ways that reflect the liberation from partiality and selfish desire that the word produces?
13: katakauchatai eleos kriseos — mercy triumphs over judgment (katakauchatai eleos kriseos, literally 'mercy boasts against/over judgment'). The verb katakauchaō means 'to boast against' or 'to triumph over' — it appears in Rom 11:18 (Gentiles should not boast against the branches) and is a strong, almost combative term. Mercy is not merely the mitigating factor that reduces the penalty; it actively overwhelms judgment. The same word appears in James 3:14: James uses it for self-glorification that overrides truth. Here mercy is the active force that overrides the judgment-consequence for those who have shown it.
15: gymnoi hyparchōsin kai leipomenoi tēs ephēmerou trophēs — naked (gymnoi — without adequate clothing, not literally unclothed) and lacking (leipomenoi, the same verb as 1:4 — deficient, short of what is needed) daily food (ephēmerou trophēs — provision for the day, daily bread). The example is deliberately domestic: not the dramatic poor but the everyday need for clothing and food. The community James describes contains both the wealthy (2:1-7) and those with these basic needs unmet — the contrast underscores the practical urgency of works.
16: hypagete en eirēnē — go in peace (hypagete en eirēnē) — the standard Jewish farewell (shalom, peace be with you), now rendered hollow by the refusal to provide physical necessities. The phrase exposes the gap between religious speech and enacted care: the blessing is real vocabulary but empty of the substance that would make it a genuine blessing. Ta epitēdeia tou sōmatos (the necessities of the body) — the word epitēdeia is a plural noun for necessary provisions; the body's needs are concrete and cannot be met by correct speech.
17: nekra kath' heautēn — the claim that faith without works is dead (nekra) 'in itself' (kath' heautēn) — by itself, alone, considered in isolation. The contrast is not between a dead faith and no faith but between faith that stands alone (without works) and faith that is accompanied by works. The word nekra is the same root as the death that sin produces in 1:15; James is drawing on the same life/death vocabulary. A dead faith is not merely ineffective; it lacks the animating principle (works = evidence of genuine trust) that makes it alive.
18: deiksō ek tōn ergōn mou tēn pistin — show me your faith apart from works and I will show you my faith by my works (deiksō ek tōn ergōn mou tēn pistin mou). The Greek makes the point epistemologically: faith is not directly observable; works are. The only way to demonstrate faith is through its visible expression in action. Chōris tōn ergōn (apart from/without works) — chōris means separated from, spatially apart. A faith spatially removed from any evidence in action is not demonstrable, hence not faith.
19: heis estin ho theos — 'there is one God' (heis estin ho theos) — the Shema (Deut 6:4: shema yisrael YHWH elohenu YHWH echad). The monotheistic confession is the foundational Jewish creed; James uses it to cut off a certain kind of boast: assenting to the Shema is not itself the distinguishing mark of genuine faith, since the demons (daimonia) do the same. The word phrisso (shudder/tremble) is vivid — the hair standing up on end in terror. The demons' response to the Shema is more overtly visceral than that of many nominal believers: they at least exhibit physical acknowledgment of the implication of monotheism.
20: argē — faith without works is argē (idle/barren/useless). The word means literally 'not working' (a- + ergon, work), which makes the wordplay explicit: faith without 'works' (ergōn) is itself 'not-working' (argē). Some manuscripts read nekra (dead) instead of argē at this point, assimilating to v17 and v26; the word argē is the harder reading and likely original, adding a new angle: barrenness/uselessness rather than death.
21: dikaiōthē — Abraham was justified (edikaiōthē, aorist passive of dikaioō) when he offered Isaac. The aorist refers to the Genesis 22 event. This has long been compared to Paul's use of the same term for Abraham in Rom 4:3 (citing Gen 15:6). The key distinction is that James uses dikaioō for a visible vindication that followed from works, while Paul uses it for a forensic declaration that preceded circumcision and preceded any work. They are not using the word in an identical sense: Paul's justification is the initial declaration; James's is the public demonstration/vindication of an already-established righteousness. Neither denies the other's point.
22: synērgei — his faith was working together with (synērgei, imperfect active of synergei — to work together, cooperate) his works. The imperfect tense depicts continuous cooperative action during the testing. The works did not produce the faith or replace it; they 'co-worked' with it. Eteleiōthē (was made complete/perfected, aorist passive of teleioō) — faith reached its completion through the works. This picks up the teleios theme from 1:4: the maturity/completeness that endurance produces is precisely what faith becomes when it works out its logic in action.
23: eplērōthē hē graphē — the scripture was fulfilled (eplērōthē, aorist passive of plēroō) that says Abraham believed God and it was reckoned to him as righteousness. The citation is Gen 15:6, the same text Paul cites in Gal 3 and Rom 4. James's point is that the Gen 22 event (Abraham's offering of Isaac) was the fulfillment of the Gen 15:6 declaration — the initial reckoning of faith as righteousness reached its intended fullness (the meaning of plēroō) when faith was enacted under test. The philos theou (friend of God) title for Abraham is from Isa 41:8 and was a standard Jewish honorific.
24: ex ergōn dikaioutai... ouk ek pisteōs monon — justified by works and not by faith alone (ouk ek pisteōs monon). The adverb monon (alone) is the key: James does not say faith is irrelevant, but that faith alone — isolated from works — is not the ground of justification in the sense of visible vindication. The ex ergōn (from works) describes the evidentiary basis by which one is 'declared righteous' before the community or in the final judgment; Paul's ek pisteōs (from faith) describes the forensic basis before God at the initial moment of justification.
25: Hraab hē pornē — Rahab the prostitute (hē pornē) — the Greek word pornē is the standard term for a commercial sex worker. James does not soften the descriptor; it is part of the argument: the unlikeliest of people is declared righteous by her works. Hypodexaménē (having received/welcomed — a hospitality term) and ekballousa (having sent away by a different route) are the two acts. The faith behind them is implied but not stated; James assumes the reader knows the Rahab narrative (Josh 2; 6:17-25) and its repeated emphasis on her trust in YHWH (Josh 2:9-11).
26: chōris pneumatos nekron... chōris tōn ergōn nekra — as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead. The analogy uses pneuma for the animating principle of the body — the breath/spirit of life (Gen 2:7: God breathed into Adam's nostrils the breath of life). Works are to faith as breath is to body: the evidence of life, not the source of it but the sign that the animating principle is present. Without breath, the body is a corpse; without works, the faith-claim is a corpse.
